---
title: "Chapter 21 — 🌫️ Diffusion Models"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 20 · 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows](ch20.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [22 · 🔗 Graph Neural Networks →](ch22.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Diffusion models are the engine behind most modern image, video, and audio generation — the technology inside systems like Stable Diffusion, DALL·E, Midjourney, and Sora. The core idea is almost paradoxically simple: teach a network to remove a little noise from a corrupted signal, then run that denoiser over and over until pure noise turns into a coherent picture. They sit in the generative-modeling corner of deep learning, alongside GANs and VAEs, but have largely displaced them for high-fidelity synthesis because they are stable to train and produce remarkably diverse, sharp outputs.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Deep generative modeling · used for text-to-image, video, and audio synthesis · the one key idea: learn to reverse a gradual noising process by repeatedly predicting and subtracting noise.
:::

## 21.1 — The forward process: destroying structure on purpose

The trick that makes diffusion work is that we never ask the model to imagine an image from nothing in one shot. Instead, we define a fixed, dumb process that slowly *destroys* an image by adding noise, and then we train a network to undo one small step of that destruction. Because the destruction is gradual and well-understood, the undoing can be learned reliably.

The **forward process** (also called the *diffusion* or *noising* process) takes a clean data point $x_0$ — say, a photo — and adds a small amount of Gaussian noise at each of $T$ steps, producing a sequence $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_T$ where $x_T$ is essentially indistinguishable from pure random noise. Each step is defined by a **variance schedule** $\beta_t$, a small number that controls how much noise gets injected:

$$q(x_t \mid x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(x_t;\ \sqrt{1-\beta_t}\,x_{t-1},\ \beta_t \mathbf{I}\right)$$

The mean is the previous image *shrunk* slightly toward zero, and a dab of fresh noise is added. Do this hundreds of times and the original signal washes out completely.

The beautiful property is that we don't have to simulate all $T$ steps to get $x_t$. Because Gaussians compose, there is a **closed form** that jumps directly from $x_0$ to any step $t$. Define $\alpha_t = 1-\beta_t$ and $\bar\alpha_t = \prod_{s=1}^{t}\alpha_s$ (the cumulative product). Then:

$$x_t = \sqrt{\bar\alpha_t}\,x_0 + \sqrt{1-\bar\alpha_t}\,\epsilon, \qquad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \mathbf{I})$$

This says: any noisy version $x_t$ is just a weighted blend of the original image and a single noise sample $\epsilon$. As $t$ grows, $\bar\alpha_t \to 0$, so the image vanishes and only noise remains. This shortcut is what makes training cheap — we can sample a random timestep, instantly produce $x_t$, and train on it.

```python
import numpy as np

T = 1000
betas = np.linspace(1e-4, 0.02, T)      # linear schedule (original DDPM)
alphas = 1.0 - betas
abar = np.cumprod(alphas)               # cumulative product alpha-bar

def q_sample(x0, t, eps):
    # jump straight to step t in closed form
    return np.sqrt(abar[t]) * x0 + np.sqrt(1 - abar[t]) * eps
```

The linear schedule above is the original DDPM choice. Modern systems usually replace it with the **cosine schedule** (Nichol & Dhariwal), which destroys structure more slowly at the start and end, spends fewer steps on near-pure noise, and noticeably improves sample quality — it is the default in most current pipelines.

::: {.callout-tip}
The whole design hinges on steps being *small*. Tiny noise per step means each reverse step is a nearly-Gaussian, nearly-linear correction the network can actually learn. Big jumps would force the model to hallucinate too much at once.
:::

## 21.2 — The reverse process and the training objective

Generating a sample means running the arrow backwards: start from pure noise $x_T$ and gradually remove noise until a clean $x_0$ emerges. The catch is that the true reverse step $q(x_{t-1}\mid x_t)$ depends on the entire data distribution and is intractable. So we approximate it with a neural network $p_\theta(x_{t-1}\mid x_t)$, which is itself modeled as a Gaussian. The network predicts that Gaussian's *mean*; the *variance* is, in the original DDPM, not learned at all but simply **fixed** to a schedule constant ($\beta_t$ or the closely related $\tilde\beta_t$). Later work lets the network learn the variance too, but the headline fact is that the network's real job is to nail the mean.

Here is the elegant part. You might expect the network to predict the slightly-cleaner image $x_{t-1}$ directly. Instead, the standard formulation (from the **DDPM** paper, *Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models*) reparameterizes the problem so the network predicts the **noise** $\epsilon$ that was added. Recall $x_t = \sqrt{\bar\alpha_t}\,x_0 + \sqrt{1-\bar\alpha_t}\,\epsilon$. If the network can guess $\epsilon$ from $x_t$, we can algebraically recover an estimate of $x_0$, and from that, the proper mean for the reverse step.

This collapses the whole derivation into a stunningly simple loss — a plain mean-squared error between the true noise and the predicted noise $\epsilon_\theta$:

$$\mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}_{x_0,\ t,\ \epsilon}\left[\big\|\,\epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(x_t, t)\,\big\|^2\right]$$

The training loop is then almost trivial:

- Pick a clean example $x_0$ from the dataset.
- Sample a random timestep $t$ and random noise $\epsilon$.
- Form $x_t$ via the closed-form forward step.
- Ask the network to predict $\epsilon$ from $(x_t, t)$, and minimize the squared error.

```python
def train_step(model, x0):
    t = np.random.randint(0, T)              # random diffusion step
    eps = np.random.randn(*x0.shape)          # the noise we'll add
    xt = q_sample(x0, t, eps)                 # noisy image at step t
    eps_pred = model(xt, t)                   # network predicts the noise
    loss = np.mean((eps - eps_pred) ** 2)     # simple MSE objective
    return loss
```

Predicting the noise $\epsilon$ is the original and most common choice, but it is not the only one. There are three equivalent **parameterizations** of the same target, and they differ only in what the network is asked to output: predict the noise $\epsilon$, predict the clean image $x_0$ directly, or predict a velocity-like blend of the two called **$v$-prediction** ($v = \sqrt{\bar\alpha_t}\,\epsilon - \sqrt{1-\bar\alpha_t}\,x_0$). The $v$ target behaves well across all noise levels and is what Stable Diffusion's later versions, most distillation methods, and the majority of video models now use. All three reconstruct the same reverse step; they are just numerically better-conditioned in different regimes.

The network itself is almost always a **U-Net**: a convolutional encoder–decoder with skip connections, conditioned on the timestep $t$ (embedded like a positional encoding) so it knows *how noisy* the input is. The timestep matters because removing noise from a faint, nearly-formed image is a very different job from carving structure out of near-pure static.

::: {.callout-warning}
A common misconception is that the model "denoises in one pass." It predicts the noise *for the current step only*. Subtracting all the predicted noise at once gives a blurry, averaged mess — the magic comes from taking many small reverse steps, each re-injecting a little randomness.
:::

## 21.3 — The sampling loop: predict, subtract, re-noise

Training gives us a network that estimates the noise. **Sampling** turns that into images by walking the reverse chain one step at a time. Each step does three things: predict the noise in the current $x_t$, subtract a calibrated fraction of it to get a slightly cleaner image, then add back a *smaller* amount of fresh noise to keep the trajectory stochastic. Concretely, the DDPM reverse-step update is:

$$x_{t-1} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_t}}\!\left(x_t - \frac{\beta_t}{\sqrt{1-\bar\alpha_t}}\,\epsilon_\theta(x_t, t)\right) + \sigma_t\, z, \qquad z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \mathbf{I})$$

The big parenthesized term is the predicted mean of the cleaner image; $\sigma_t$ (typically $\sqrt{\beta_t}$) is the re-injected noise, set to zero on the final step so the output is clean. This single equation is the central mechanic of the whole chapter — every sampler in §21.5 is a variation on how to take this step.

```python
def p_sample(model, xt, t):
    eps = model(xt, t)                                  # predict the noise
    mean = (xt - betas[t] / np.sqrt(1 - abar[t]) * eps) / np.sqrt(alphas[t])
    if t == 0:
        return mean                                     # last step: no noise
    z = np.random.randn(*xt.shape)
    return mean + np.sqrt(betas[t]) * z                 # re-inject a little noise
```

That re-injection is what lets the same starting noise explore different plausible images and avoids collapse into a single blurry average. Run `p_sample` from $t=T-1$ down to $t=0$, starting from $x_T \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\mathbf{I})$, and a coherent image emerges.

## 21.4 — The noise ⇄ denoise loop

It helps to see the two processes side by side. The forward chain is fixed and requires no learning; the reverse chain is where the neural network lives.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    x0["x₀ clean image"] -->|"+ noise β₁"| x1["x₁"]
    x1 -->|"+ noise"| xdots["…"]
    xdots -->|"+ noise β_T"| xT["x_T ≈ pure noise"]
    xT -.->|"εθ predict & remove"| ydots["…"]
    ydots -.->|"εθ"| y1["x₁"]
    y1 -.->|"εθ"| y0["x₀ generated image"]
    subgraph FWD["Forward — fixed, no learning"]
        x0
        x1
        xdots
        xT
    end
    subgraph REV["Reverse — learned U-Net εθ"]
        xT
        ydots
        y1
        y0
    end
```

Reading it as a loop: solid arrows going right add noise (training data is generated for free this way); dashed arrows going left are the learned denoiser applied repeatedly at sampling time. Each reverse step runs the update from §21.3. The forward direction never touches the network — it exists purely to manufacture $(x_t, \epsilon)$ training pairs on demand.

## 21.5 — The score-based view and SDEs

There is a second, deeper lens on diffusion that unifies it with a whole family of methods. Instead of thinking about discrete noising steps, picture a continuous flow of probability over time, governed by a **stochastic differential equation (SDE)**. The forward SDE smoothly pushes the data distribution toward a simple Gaussian; the remarkable result (from Song et al.) is that this process has a corresponding *reverse* SDE that turns the Gaussian back into data — and the only unknown quantity it needs is the **score**.

The score is the gradient of the log-density, $\nabla_x \log p(x)$ — a vector field that, at any point in space, points toward regions of higher data probability. If you know which way is "more like real data" everywhere, you can follow that signal uphill to generate samples. **Score-based models** train a network to estimate this field at every noise level, a technique called *score matching*.

The connection to DDPM is direct, not coincidental: predicting the noise $\epsilon_\theta$ is, up to a scaling factor, the same as predicting the score. Specifically,

$$\nabla_{x_t} \log p(x_t) \approx -\frac{\epsilon_\theta(x_t, t)}{\sqrt{1-\bar\alpha_t}}$$

The minus sign carries the intuition: the noise vector $\epsilon$ points *away* from the data — it is exactly the corruption that dragged $x_0$ off the data manifold — so the score, which points *toward* higher-probability data, is its negation. Generating a sample is following $-\epsilon_\theta$ back uphill. This view matters because it lets us borrow the mathematics of differential equations: sampling becomes numerically *integrating* the reverse SDE (or its deterministic cousin, the **probability-flow ODE**), which opens the door to faster, more principled samplers.

## 21.6 — Samplers: trading steps for speed

The original DDPM sampler is faithful but slow: it walks all $T$ steps (often 1000), running the U-Net once per step. That is a thousand forward passes for a single image. Most practical work on diffusion sampling is about getting comparable quality in far fewer steps.

**DDPM** sampling is stochastic — it adds fresh noise at every reverse step (the $\sigma_t z$ term from §21.3), following the Markov chain exactly. **DDIM** (*Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models*) reinterprets the same trained model under a *non-Markovian* process whose reverse step can be made fully **deterministic** (the $\sigma_t z$ term goes to zero). Once deterministic, you can skip steps: jump from step 1000 straight to 980 to 960, mapping the same starting noise to the same image with as few as 20–50 steps. Because it's an ODE integrator at heart, DDIM is also what makes operations like smoothly interpolating between two images possible.

| Aspect | DDPM | DDIM |
|---|---|---|
| Reverse process | Stochastic (adds noise each step) | Deterministic (no added noise) |
| Typical steps | ~1000 | 20–50 |
| Same seed → same image? | No (random each run) | Yes (reproducible) |
| Source of diversity | Re-injected noise at every step | Only the initial-noise prior |
| Speed | Slow | Much faster |

Beyond these two, modern **ODE solvers** (DPM-Solver, and higher-order methods) push quality images down to roughly 10–20 steps by applying smarter numerical integration to the probability-flow ODE. The general tradeoff is intuitive: fewer steps means faster generation but coarser approximation of the reverse trajectory, which can blur fine detail or miss small structures. Practitioners tune the step count to the budget — many steps for a final hero image, a handful for fast previews.

::: {.callout-tip}
Rule of thumb: if you need *reproducible* outputs or *interpolation* between samples, reach for a deterministic sampler (DDIM/ODE). With DDIM a fixed seed always gives the same image, so all variety comes from changing the initial noise. Stochastic DDPM re-rolls noise at every step, so it explores more from the same seed.
:::

## 21.7 — Conditioning and classifier-free guidance

So far the model generates *something* from the data distribution, but we usually want control — "a photo of a corgi on a skateboard," not a random image. **Conditioning** means feeding the network an extra signal $c$ (a class label, a text embedding, a depth map) so it predicts $\epsilon_\theta(x_t, t, c)$, steering the denoising toward outputs consistent with $c$.

The early approach, **classifier guidance**, used a separate image classifier and nudged each reverse step along the gradient of "how much this looks like class $c$." It worked but required training an extra noise-aware classifier — clunky and an extra moving part.

**Classifier-free guidance (CFG)** is the elegant replacement that nearly all modern systems use. The idea: train a *single* model that sometimes sees the real condition $c$ and sometimes sees a null/empty condition $\varnothing$ (achieved by randomly dropping the conditioning during training, say 10% of the time). At sampling, you run the model twice — once conditioned, once unconditioned — and *extrapolate* in the direction that the condition pushes:

$$\hat\epsilon = \epsilon_\theta(x_t, t, \varnothing) + s\,\big(\epsilon_\theta(x_t, t, c) - \epsilon_\theta(x_t, t, \varnothing)\big)$$

Geometrically, you are not interpolating between the two predictions but pushing *past* the conditional one, away from the unconditional:

<svg viewBox="0 0 420 80" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <line x1="30" y1="50" x2="390" y2="50" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <circle cx="70" cy="50" r="5" fill="currentColor"/>
  <text x="70" y="38" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle" fill="currentColor">∅ uncond</text>
  <circle cx="200" cy="50" r="5" fill="currentColor"/>
  <text x="200" y="38" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle" fill="currentColor">c cond (s=1)</text>
  <circle cx="350" cy="50" r="5" fill="currentColor"/>
  <text x="350" y="38" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle" fill="currentColor">ŝ extrapolated</text>
  <polygon points="390,50 380,46 380,54" fill="currentColor"/>
  <text x="320" y="70" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="currentColor">higher s →</text>
</svg>

The **guidance scale** $s$ is the knob. At $s=1$ you get ordinary conditional generation. Crank $s$ up (commonly 5–15 for text-to-image) and the output adheres far more strongly to the prompt, at the cost of less diversity and, if pushed too hard, oversaturated or unnatural-looking images.

The same machinery powers the **negative prompt** — a text-to-image user's most common control. Instead of leaving the unconditional slot empty, you replace $\varnothing$ with the embedding of a concept you *don't* want ("blurry, extra fingers, watermark"). CFG then extrapolates *away* from that unwanted concept and toward your real prompt, actively pushing the named flaws out of the image.

::: {.callout-warning}
Higher guidance is not "better quality," it's *stronger prompt adherence*. Excessive CFG scales produce garish, over-contrasted images and collapse variety — a frequent beginner mistake when prompts "aren't working." Often the fix is lowering the scale, not raising it.
:::

## 21.8 — Latent diffusion and text-to-image pipelines

Running diffusion directly on pixels is brutally expensive: a 512×512 RGB image is ~790,000 values, and the U-Net must process that hundreds of times per generation. **Latent diffusion** — the architecture behind **Stable Diffusion** — sidesteps this with a simple insight: do the diffusion in a *compressed* space, not in pixel space.

First, a separately trained **autoencoder** (a VAE) compresses images into a much smaller **latent** representation — typically downsampling 512×512 pixels by a factor of 8 per side to a 64×64 latent grid with 4 channels. That is $\tfrac{512^2 \times 3}{64^2 \times 4} = 48\times$ fewer values to diffuse over (8× spatial downsampling per side, but 4 latent channels instead of 3 RGB). The diffusion model then learns to denoise *latents* instead of pixels. After sampling a clean latent, the autoencoder's decoder expands it back into a full image. Because perceptually important structure survives compression while imperceptible high-frequency detail is offloaded to the decoder, the diffusion model can focus its capacity on content rather than pixel minutiae — and runs an order of magnitude cheaper.

Bolting on text gives the full **text-to-image** pipeline. A pretrained **text encoder** (such as CLIP's text tower) turns the prompt into a sequence of embeddings. The U-Net attends to these embeddings via **cross-attention** layers at every resolution, so the text influences denoising throughout. Combined with classifier-free guidance on the text condition (and, optionally, a negative prompt), this is the recipe for prompt-driven generation.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    prompt["text prompt"] --> te["text encoder (CLIP)"]
    te --> emb["text embeddings"]
    noise["random latent z_T"] --> unet["U-Net denoiser"]
    emb -->|"cross-attention"| unet
    unet -->|"iterate reverse steps"| z0["clean latent z₀"]
    z0 --> dec["VAE decoder"]
    dec --> img["output image"]
```

The end-to-end flow at generation time: encode the prompt, sample a random latent, run the conditioned U-Net for $N$ reverse steps (with CFG and a chosen sampler), then decode the final latent to pixels. Every component we have built — forward/reverse process, noise-prediction loss, samplers, conditioning, guidance — comes together here. The same template generalizes to video (add temporal layers) and audio (diffuse over spectrograms or waveforms).

## 21.9 — Why diffusion overtook GANs

For years, **GANs** (Chapter 20) were the state of the art in image synthesis, but they came with well-known pains. GAN training is a delicate adversarial min-max game prone to instability and **mode collapse**, where the generator learns to produce only a few convincing outputs and ignores the diversity of the real data. Getting a GAN to train at high resolution required a bag of tricks and careful tuning.

Diffusion models fixed the two biggest problems at once. Their training objective is a plain, stable regression — predict the noise — with no adversary, no balancing act, and a single loss that reliably goes down. And because every sample traces an independent path from fresh random noise through the full reverse process, they cover the data distribution far more completely, yielding striking **diversity** and **mode coverage** rather than collapsing to a handful of templates.

| | GANs | Diffusion |
|---|---|---|
| Training | Adversarial, unstable | Single regression loss, stable |
| Mode coverage | Prone to collapse | Broad, diverse |
| Sampling speed | One forward pass (fast) | Many steps (slower) |
| Controllability | Harder to condition cleanly | Cross-attention + CFG, very flexible |

The one genuine drawback is speed: a GAN generates in a single forward pass, while diffusion needs many. That gap is exactly why the field has poured effort into few-step samplers and **distillation** (training a fast student to mimic the multi-step teacher in one or a few steps). The verdict for high-fidelity, controllable image, video, and audio generation has been decisive — diffusion won on stability, diversity, and the ease of plugging in rich conditioning.

## 21.x — Key takeaways

- Diffusion defines a **fixed forward noising process** and learns a **neural reverse process** that removes noise step by step; the closed-form forward jump makes training cheap. The **cosine schedule** has largely replaced the original linear one.
- The training objective is simply to **predict the noise** $\epsilon$ added at a random timestep, optimized with plain MSE. Three equivalent parameterizations exist — predict $\epsilon$, $x_0$, or $v$ — with **$v$-prediction** now standard in newer and video models.
- Sampling repeats one update: **predict noise, subtract it, re-inject a little fresh noise** ($x_{t-1} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_t}}(x_t - \frac{\beta_t}{\sqrt{1-\bar\alpha_t}}\epsilon_\theta) + \sigma_t z$). In DDPM the reverse **variance is fixed**, not learned; the network predicts only the mean.
- Predicting noise is equivalent to learning the **score** $\nabla_x \log p(x)$ (negated and scaled); the continuous **SDE/ODE** view unifies diffusion with score-based models and enables principled samplers.
- **Samplers** trade steps for speed: DDPM is stochastic and slow (~1000 steps), DDIM is deterministic and fast (20–50 steps, diversity from the initial noise only), and ODE solvers push lower still.
- **Classifier-free guidance** steers generation by extrapolating between conditional and unconditional predictions; the guidance scale controls prompt adherence vs. diversity, and swapping the null condition for an unwanted concept gives the **negative prompt**.
- **Latent diffusion** (Stable Diffusion) runs the process in a compressed VAE latent space (~48× fewer values); text-to-image adds a **text encoder** feeding the U-Net via **cross-attention**.
- Diffusion **overtook GANs** for synthesis by being stable to train and far better at mode coverage, at the cost of slower multi-step sampling.

## 21.y — See also

- **Chapter 20 — Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows** — the generative families diffusion competes with, and the VAE that powers latent diffusion.
- **Chapter 16 — Convolutional Neural Networks** — the U-Net backbone and the convolutional machinery inside the denoiser.
- **Chapter 18 — Attention & Transformers** — cross-attention, the mechanism that injects text conditioning into the U-Net.
- **Chapter 19 — Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning** — the encoder/decoder compression that latent diffusion relies on.
- **Chapter 34 — Multimodal AI** — text encoders like CLIP and the broader landscape of text-to-image and text-to-video systems.
- **Chapter 02 — Mathematics for Machine Learning** — the Gaussians, gradients, and differential-equation background behind the score-based view.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 20 · 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows](ch20.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [22 · 🔗 Graph Neural Networks →](ch22.html)
